name: Real Essay Comparisons Reveal Tradeoffs Across Score Agreement and Realism Detection
statement: Wolfe and Barber compare simulated essays against the original real student essays and show that agreement, average-score alignment, and human detection produce different judgments about synthetic quality.
population_or_context: Student essay responses generated for automated-scoring data augmentation.
time_horizon: arXiv preprint submitted 2026-02-06 and reviewed on 2026-06-02.
quantitative_note: Table 2 reports Predict Next at 0.72 exact agreement and 0.74 quadratic weighted kappa, Sentence at an average score of 1.97 versus 1.53 for original essays, and simulation-detection accuracy of 0.18 for 25 Examples, 0.25 for Predict Next, and 0.74 for Sentence.
caveats: The real essay comparator is also the source essay used to condition generation.
Realism and score preservation can diverge.
evaluation_method: Expert scoring, score-distribution comparison, exact agreement, quadratic weighted kappa, and simulation-detection judgments.
reported_metrics: Average score, agreement with original student score, quadratic weighted kappa, and simulation-detection accuracy by prompt strategy.
limitations: The study does not validate automated scorers solely on synthetic essays.

source_locator.quote_or_paraphrase_note: The finding paraphrases the Table 2 simulated-versus-real essay comparison.
